metric_name: IDC core belief — application-level availability not negotiable
metric_value: There is no place in the datacenter for lower availability at the application level simply because the storage uses capacity-oriented technology
notes: Key adoption constraint — SATA must be made reliable at application level regardless of drive-level failure rates

metric_value: Most receptive applications are (1) mirrors and snapshots for data protection and (2) repositories for fixed content
notes: PREDICTION: Data protection and fixed content would be the dominant SATA use cases — confirmed

metric_value: Capacity-oriented technology will be embedded within arrays as a class of capacity managed through current tools
notes: PREDICTION: SATA would be integrated into existing array products not sold as separate arrays — confirmed

metric_name: SATA market share prediction — TB shipments
metric_value: Over time capacity-oriented storage shipments will reach a quarter or more of the storage array terabytes shipped
notes: PREDICTION: SATA ≥25% of new storage TB shipments — substantially exceeded; SATA >50% by 2010

metric_value: [DEFERRED]
notes: SATA HDDs became dominant in secondary/nearline storage by 2008-2010 — Phase 3 verification

metric_name: Multiple SATA product tiers not viable
metric_value: There is not currently enough space for multiple classes/tiers of storage products based on SATA — market should be analyzed as single tier
notes: PREDICTION: Against multiple SATA product tiers — this was conservative; SATA + SAS later created two-tier within capacity storage

metric_name: SATA threat to tape — minimal near term
metric_value: Capacity-oriented disk storage poses little near or medium term threat to tape; in fact larger disk capacity means more data to transfer to tape for archiving
notes: PREDICTION: SATA not a tape killer in near/medium term — confirmed; tape remained strong for deep archive

metric_value: Lack of effective information management software and concurrent lack of information policies will be the major inhibitor for aggressive adoption of tiered storage
notes: PREDICTION: Software gap would slow SATA/tiered storage adoption — confirmed; ILM software remained immature through 2008

metric_value: 50 North American in-depth one-hour interviews conducted October-November 2003 across 10 vertical markets (banking/energy/financial/health/insurance/manufacturing/media/pharmaceutical/transportation/wholesale retail)
notes: Sponsored by vendors at storage semiconductor disk drive and storage systems levels

metric_value: Spending on IT in general and storage in specific expected to increase in 2004 — majority expect storage growth
notes: Most organizations expected modest storage budget increases in 2004

metric_value: Raw survey results suggest 14% of all storage dollars in 2004 would be on SATA/high-capacity low-cost storage
notes: IDC characterizes this as 'ideal/perfect world' scenario and tempers with conservative adjustment

metric_value: Number one SATA concern mentioned 30% of time: less reliable than SCSI or FC; performance second; 'too new' tied for second
notes: Stigma of low reliability as primary adoption barrier — must be overcome through vendor reputation and RAID

metric_value: HP most preferred SATA storage supplier among 19 interviewees surveyed; 10 of 19 mentions; IBM and EMC also mentioned
notes: Question inserted mid-way through interviews so only 19 respondents; HP dominance reflects incumbent storage vendor trust

metric_name: Vertical market TAM ranking — manufacturing first
metric_value: Manufacturing has highest TAM for capacity-oriented storage ($802M); Financial ($733M) second; Transportation ($477M) third
notes: Note: Energy and Pharmaceutical are derivative markets partly double-counted

metric_name: Vertical market adoption density — financial first
metric_value: By percent of installed base OK for capacity-oriented storage: Financial 51.9% (1st); Transportation 33.1% (2nd); Media 32.7% (3rd)
notes: Financial sector most receptive by proportion of existing storage suitable for SATA

metric_name: Compliance verticals as long-term tape conversion targets
metric_value: Industries with compliance requirements (Financial/Health/Pharmaceutical) have highest ratio of near-line tape to disk — highest long-term conversion opportunity for SATA
notes: Health ratio: 9.75 TB tape per disk TB; Pharmaceutical: 6.47; Financial: 5.00

metric_name: Data protection — most popular SATA application
metric_value: Data protection (mirroring/snapshots/rapid restore) is most popular use for SATA; 29% of total installed base TBs targeted; many already engaged
notes: Largely additive business not substitutive — good news for storage industry

metric_name: Fixed content SATA adoption — 12% of installed base
metric_value: 12% of total installed base TBs estimated as appropriate for fixed-content management applications — IDC believes this is low and will grow with compliance
notes: Compliance regulations (HIPAA/SOX/WORM requirements) will drive fixed content SATA adoption upward

metric_value: 60% of all reported installed terabytes (4563 TB total sample) are considered over-served — appropriate for capacity-oriented storage
notes: Pharmaceutical most over-served at 77.6%; Wholesale/Retail at 124% (more suitable TBs than installed — skewed by one large retailer)

metric_value: 4563 TB total enterprise storage across 49 qualifying interviews (one disqualified); grand total by vertical market
notes: Large organizations with 10+ TB of enterprise storage and existing SAN — purposive sample of leading-edge users

metric_name: WW storage system revenue forecast 2003-2007 (internal + external)
metric_value: Total internal storage revenue: $6.8B in 2003 flat through 2007 (0.4% CAGR); TB growth: 364K TB (2003) to 1.29M TB (2007) at 37.2% CAGR; $/GB declining from $19 to $5
notes: Revenue flat but capacity (TB) growing dramatically — price/performance curve drives SATA adoption

metric_value: Internal ATA storage revenue: $283M (2003) growing to $313M (2007) at 2.5% CAGR; share 4.2% to 4.5%
notes: Conservative near-term forecast for ATA in internal storage — most SATA opportunity in external arrays

metric_name: SATA market growth toward 50% of new capacity
metric_value: Market is moving now and can grow toward 50% of new capacity (per executive summary heading)
notes: PREDICTION: SATA could reach 50% of new capacity — exceeded by ~2010

metric_name: SATA 50% of new storage capacity outcome
metric_value: [DEFERRED]
notes: Phase 3 verification — IDC/Gartner data should confirm when SATA HDDs exceeded 50% of new enterprise storage capacity TB shipped

metric_value: Strong tendency in corporate IT to lean toward status quo rather than adopt fundamentally different technology; ATA in enterprise is fundamental departure from SCSI/FC status quo
notes: Conservative adjustment applied to raw survey adoption intentions because of this structural tendency

metric_name: SAS unknown to most storage managers
metric_value: Most storage managers not aware of SAS technology — education required; combined value proposition of SATA and SAS together is unknown
notes: SAS was barely entering market in late 2003; SATA/SAS co-existence in arrays not yet understood

metric_name: Organizations to avoid for SATA sales
metric_value: Avoid: insurance industry; public sector without oversight or competition; highly profitable niche markets without cost pressures — hostile to capacity-oriented story
notes: Insurance ranked last in both TAM and adoption density — confirmed by IDC data showing 8% acceptance rate

metric_value: Organizations with growing storage under cost pressure especially those with compliance requirements requiring permanent retention and instant retrieval: Financial/Transportation/Pharmaceutical
notes: Strong convergence with Aberdeen-Maxtor survey target verticals

metric_name: Array integration preferred over standalone SATA appliances
metric_value: Biggest market is for classes of capacity within storage arrays currently being sold; only minority want separate array products
notes: PREDICTION: SATA would be adopted as embedded tier in existing array architectures — confirmed

metric_name: RAID-5 and application-level availability non-negotiable for SATA
metric_value: Data integrity requires RAID-5 for SATA systems; no compromise in uptime acceptable; IDC recommends products withstand 2 drive failures without application interruption
notes: Technical requirement that drives up SATA system cost and complexity — but still achievable at 50% cost savings

metric_name: EMC Centera performance vs conventional RAID
metric_value: Centera reported at application level to be one-third the performance of conventional RAID-based architecture by enterprise interviewees; indexing features reported missing
notes: Early negative datapoint for ATA-based fixed content storage — performance gap needs to close

metric_value: Most organizations lack active information management policies; everyone considers their data most important; no incentives in place; some considering charge-back models for storage
notes: Policy gap is structural inhibitor that software alone cannot fix — requires organizational change

metric_value: Compliance requirements are setting the stage; organizations with clear tiering and compliance efforts already implementing document management for compliance; this will be first source level of metadata for true data tiering and ILM
notes: PREDICTION: Compliance-driven adoption would precede and enable broader ILM — confirmed by HIPAA/SOX/WORM storage adoption

metric_value: [DEFERRED]
notes: Phase 3 verification — HIPAA and SOX compliance storage became a major market segment by 2005-2007

metric_value: Functional SANs now 3+ years old; users actively looking for better value; many transforming one-size-fits-all storage strategy; tier-2 applications being migrated to appropriate arrays
notes: Confirms that the SAN market is mature enough to add tiering — timing is right for midline adoption
